messages.content:
You are an adaptive remediation agent on Rocky Linux / RHEL. Use tools to inspect and remediate the finding.
STRATEGY:
1. ALWAYS read the relevant config file FIRST with read_file before making changes.
2. Identify the exact key/value that needs changing.
3. Use write_file for config changes (avoids shell quoting issues) or run_cmd for sed/systemctl.
4. Verify with run_cmd (e.g. grep) that the change took effect.
5. A verification scan runs automatically after your session — do not call scan yourself.
RULES:
- One command at a time. Do NOT chain with && or ;
- This is Rocky Linux/RHEL. Use dnf (not apt). Use systemctl (not service).
- Do NOT duplicate config lines. If a key already exists, modify it in-place with sed.
- If a key is commented out (# minlen = 8), uncomment and set the correct value.
- stderr may contain SSH banners — ignore them. Check exit_code and stdout for results.
- CRITICAL: Do NOT modify SSH config, sshd_config, firewall rules, or SELinux in ways that could block SSH access. Do not disable or mask sshd. The pipeline requires SSH to operate.

You are the Remedy agent remediating ONE OpenSCAP finding on Rocky Linux 10.
You MUST use ONLY the provided tools: run_cmd, read_file, write_file, scan.
Rules:
- Use run_cmd for EXACTLY ONE shell command at a time. Do NOT chain with && ; or multiline scripts.
- Commands run as root. Do not prefix with sudo.
- ALWAYS use read_file to inspect the target config file BEFORE modifying it.
- Do NOT append duplicate lines. Use sed -i to modify existing values in-place.
- If a line is commented (e.g. '# minlen = 8'), uncomment it and set the value.
- Prefer minimal, reversible changes and verify with scan at the end.

FINDING:
- Title: Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv4 Forwarding on all IPv4 Interfaces
- Rule Name: sysctl_net_ipv4_conf_all_forwarding
- Rule ID: xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_sysctl_net_ipv4_conf_all_forwarding
- Finding ID: openscap_028
- Severity: 2
- Host: localhost

TRIAGE CONTEXT:
- Approved for remediation because: The issue involves disabling IPv4 forwarding via sysctl, which can be safely remedied by editing sysctl configuration files and reloading settings; it does not affect authentication services or critical boot components, and standard remediation scripts are provided.
- Description: To set the runtime status of the net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding=0 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding = 0
- Recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_e

Return tool calls as needed. A verification scan runs automatically after your session.

Goal: Remediate OpenSCAP finding "Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv4 Forwarding on all IPv4 Interfaces" (xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_sysctl_net_ipv4_conf_all_forwarding). The rule suggests setting net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding = 0 permanently via a sysctl config file in /etc/sysctl.d.

We have created a file /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-ipv4-forwarding.conf with "net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding = 0". However, scanning still reports fail. Indeed reading /etc/sysctl.conf shows that net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding = 1 is set there, and that file is processed after /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf? Actually the order: sysctl reads /etc/sysctl.conf after /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf? I recall that files in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf are read in lexical order, and then /etc/sysctl.conf is read last, thus overriding earlier settings. So the net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding setting in /etc/sysctl.conf will override the setting in 99-disable-ipv4-forwarding.conf (since that file is processed earlier).
